verdict: All three posts and the newsletter carry genuine operational depth and translate the source framework into VRM-specific behavior accurately. The posts avoid book-report structure, include concrete examples and scripts, and dismantle real operator misconceptions. The newsletter extends the posts meaningfully rather than recapping them.
strengths: Post 1 correctly identifies the misdiagnosis (motivation vs. architecture) before introducing the framework, which is the right structure for this audience
Post 2 includes a replacement script that sounds like something a real operator would actually say, not a sales training exercise
Post 3 uses the keystone habit concept to directly address the change-overload failure mode common in VRM BD, with a concrete Oratia field note
The newsletter's organizational habit audit section (stand-up, celebration, what gets cut) is genuinely actionable and goes beyond what the posts covered
The diagnostic section in the newsletter (five-element cue identification) provides a usable tool, not abstract advice
No em dashes present across all files
Character counts are within the 2,900-character limit for all three posts
CTA footer is present and intact on all posts
problems: Post 1 opening hook 'Prospecting doesn't fail because you stop trying. It fails because you never built the loop.' is strong but the 'never built the loop' phrasing requires the reader to already know what a loop is. Consider whether this lands cleanly on first read before seeing the body.
Newsletter NEXT WEEK section references Atomic Habits as the next series, which is accurate per instructions but should be verified against actual publishing sequence
